seo.title: Education Code Generation AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for code generation AI agents in education. Covers FERPA, COPPA, State education data privacy laws.

content.intro: Deploying AI code generation agents in educational settings introduces compounded risk: student data protected under FERPA and COPPA intersects with the inherent dangers of executable code, sandbox escapes, and dependency vulnerabilities. A single misconfiguration can expose minors to harmful content, leak PII, or trigger institutional liability and accreditation review. This checklist provides concrete, education-specific controls to ensure your AI coding agent meets legal, pedagogical, and technical safety requirements before going live.

sections.heading: Student Data Privacy & Consent
sections.description: Ensure all student interactions with the code generation agent comply with FERPA, COPPA, and applicable state education data privacy laws before any data is collected or processed.

items.label: Verify Parental Consent for Users Under 13
items.description: Implement a COPPA-compliant consent gate that requires verifiable parental consent before any student under 13 can interact with the agent. Store signed consent records with timestamps and parent/guardian identity verification.

items.label: Classify and Minimize Student Data Collection
items.description: Audit every data field collected during code generation sessions (prompts, submitted code, error logs, session metadata). Retain only what is educationally necessary and document the legitimate educational interest justification required under FERPA.

items.label: Prohibit PII Injection into LLM Prompts
items.description: Implement a pre-processing filter that strips student names, student IDs, email addresses, and school identifiers from all prompts before they are sent to the LLM. Log all filter interventions for audit review.

items.label: Execute a FERPA-Compliant Data Processing Agreement with LLM Vendor
items.description: Confirm the LLM provider has signed a FERPA-compliant School Official agreement or equivalent DPA that prohibits using student interaction data for model training, advertising, or any non-educational purpose.

items.label: Implement State-Specific Data Residency Controls
items.description: Identify the data residency requirements of each state where the platform operates (e.g., California SOPIPA, New York Education Law 2-d) and configure data storage and processing to remain within compliant geographic boundaries.

items.label: Define and Enforce Data Retention and Deletion Schedules
items.description: Set automated deletion policies for student code submissions, session logs, and LLM interaction records aligned with your institution's records retention schedule. Provide a student/parent-facing data deletion request workflow.

items.label: Conduct Annual FERPA Training for All Platform Administrators
items.description: Require all staff with access to student interaction logs or agent configuration to complete FERPA training annually. Maintain training completion records and tie access provisioning to certification status.

sections.heading: Code Safety & Sandbox Execution Controls
sections.description: The code generation agent must execute student-submitted and AI-generated code in a fully isolated environment that prevents system compromise, data exfiltration, and exposure to malicious dependencies.

items.label: Enforce Strict Sandbox Isolation for All Code Execution
items.description: Run all generated code in a containerized sandbox (e.g., gVisor, Firecracker microVM) with no network access, no filesystem access outside a designated temp directory, and hard CPU/memory limits. Validate isolation on every deployment with escape-attempt test suites.

items.label: Block Dangerous System Calls and Imports at the Sandbox Level
items.description: Maintain a blocklist of prohibited Python/JS/shell constructs (e.g., os.system, subprocess, eval, exec, require('child_process')) and enforce it via static analysis before execution. Reject and flag any code containing blocked patterns.

items.label: Scan All Generated Code for Credential and Secret Leakage
items.description: Run every LLM-generated code snippet through a secrets detection tool (e.g., Trufflehog, detect-secrets) before presenting it to students. Automatically redact or block any output containing API keys, passwords, tokens, or connection strings.

items.label: Validate Dependency Safety Before Installation in Sandbox
items.description: Before installing any package suggested by the LLM (pip, npm, etc.), check it against OSV, Snyk, or NIST NVD for known CVEs. Maintain an approved package allowlist for student-facing environments and block unlisted packages by default.

items.label: Enforce Execution Time and Resource Quotas Per Student Session
items.description: Set per-session limits: maximum 30-second execution time, 256MB RAM, and 100MB disk write. Automatically terminate and log sessions that exceed thresholds to prevent denial-of-service from infinite loops or resource exhaustion.

items.label: Implement Syntax Validation Before Execution
items.description: Run a language-appropriate linter or AST parser (e.g., pylint, ESLint) on all generated code before sandbox execution. Surface syntax errors to the student with educational explanations rather than raw stack traces that could reveal system internals.

items.label: Log All Code Execution Events with Tamper-Evident Audit Trail
items.description: Record every code execution event including student session ID (anonymized), generated code hash, execution result, and any sandbox policy violations. Store logs in a write-once, append-only system accessible to institutional security teams.

items.label: Test Sandbox Escape Resistance Quarterly
items.description: Engage a security team to run a quarterly sandbox penetration test using current escape techniques (container breakouts, kernel exploits, symlink attacks). Remediate any findings within 30 days and document results for institutional risk review.

sections.heading: Age-Appropriate Content & Pedagogical Integrity
sections.description: AI-generated code examples, explanations, and feedback must be appropriate for the student's age and grade level, free from bias, and structured to support rather than replace qualified instruction.

items.label: Implement Grade-Level Content Filters on All LLM Outputs
items.description: Configure the LLM system prompt and output filtering to enforce age-appropriate language and complexity. For K-8 deployments, restrict outputs to concepts within the grade-band curriculum and block adult-oriented code examples (e.g., gambling algorithms, adult content scrapers).

items.label: Prohibit the Agent from Providing Direct Assignment Answers
items.description: Configure the agent's instructional mode to provide hints, explain concepts, and ask Socratic questions rather than generating complete solutions to assignment prompts. Implement prompt classification to detect assignment-completion requests and redirect to guided learning.

items.label: Establish Human Instructor Escalation Pathways
items.description: Define and implement triggers that escalate student interactions to a qualified human instructor: repeated confusion signals, requests involving sensitive topics, or code that suggests the student may be attempting harmful activities. Document escalation SLAs.

items.label: Audit Generated Code Examples for Algorithmic Bias
items.description: Quarterly, review a random sample of 200 generated code examples for biased variable naming, stereotyped problem scenarios, or culturally exclusionary examples. Use a rubric aligned with CSTA equity standards and remediate prompt engineering based on findings.

items.label: Align Generated Content with Adopted Curriculum Standards
items.description: Map the agent's instructional scope to the institution's adopted standards (e.g., CSTA K-12 CS Standards, AP CS Principles framework). Restrict the agent from teaching concepts outside the enrolled course scope without instructor authorization.

items.label: Provide Instructor Dashboard with Full Visibility into Student-Agent Interactions
items.description: Build an instructor-facing dashboard that shows anonymized interaction summaries, flags unusual patterns (e.g., a student receiving 20 consecutive direct answers), and allows instructors to review and override agent behavior per student.

items.label: Conduct Bias and Accuracy Review with Diverse Educator Panel Before Launch
items.description: Before deploying to students, have a panel of at least 5 educators representing diverse backgrounds review 100 sample agent interactions for accuracy, bias, and pedagogical soundness. Require sign-off from the panel before production release.

sections.heading: Accessibility, Transparency & Institutional Governance
sections.description: The agent must meet Section 508 and WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards, clearly disclose its AI nature to students, and operate under documented institutional governance with defined accountability.

items.label: Certify Section 508 and WCAG 2.1 AA Compliance for the Agent Interface
items.description: Conduct a formal accessibility audit of the code generation interface using automated tools (axe, WAVE) and manual screen reader testing (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver). Remediate all Level A and AA failures before student-facing deployment. Publish a VPAT.

items.label: Display Clear AI Disclosure to Students at Session Start
items.description: Present a plain-language disclosure at the start of every session stating that the student is interacting with an AI system, that AI can make mistakes, and that a human instructor is available. For students under 13, use age-appropriate language reviewed by a child literacy expert.

items.label: Designate an Institutional AI Accountability Owner
items.description: Formally assign a named staff member (e.g., Chief Privacy Officer or Director of Educational Technology) as the accountable owner for the AI agent. Document their responsibilities including incident response, annual review, and regulatory reporting obligations.

items.label: Publish a Student-Facing AI Use Policy and Obtain Acknowledgment
items.description: Draft and publish a student-facing AI use policy that explains what the agent does, what data is collected, how it is used, and student rights under FERPA. Require students (and parents for minors) to acknowledge the policy before first use.

items.label: Implement an Incident Response Plan Specific to AI-Related Student Data Breaches
items.description: Create a written incident response plan covering AI-specific breach scenarios: PII leakage via LLM output, sandbox escape exposing student data, or unauthorized model training on student data. Define notification timelines compliant with state breach notification laws (typically 30-72 hours).

items.label: Conduct Annual Third-Party Privacy and Security Assessment
items.description: Commission an annual independent assessment covering FERPA compliance, COPPA controls, sandbox security, and LLM vendor data handling. Share a summary of findings and remediation status with the institution's board or governing body.

items.label: Provide Accessible Alternative for Students Who Cannot Use the AI Agent
items.description: Ensure that no student is disadvantaged by inability to use the AI agent due to disability, lack of parental consent, or technology access. Document and implement an equivalent non-AI instructional pathway for all learning objectives covered by the agent.

notes.note: Any LLM vendor processing student interaction data must qualify as a 'school official' with a legitimate educational interest under FERPA, or a signed data processing agreement must explicitly prohibit secondary use of student data. Institutions must maintain a record of all third-party disclosures and make it available to parents upon request. Failure to comply can result in loss of federal funding.

For students under 13, the school may provide consent on behalf of parents only for educational purposes with no commercial use of the data. The AI agent operator must provide a direct notice to parents describing data collection practices and must honor verifiable deletion requests within a reasonable timeframe. The FTC has issued civil penalties exceeding $170M for COPPA violations.

At least 45 states have enacted student data privacy laws beyond FERPA, many of which impose stricter requirements. California's SOPIPA prohibits targeted advertising and sale of student data. New York's Education Law 2-d requires a Parents' Bill of Rights and third-party contractor data security obligations. Operators must conduct a state-by-state compliance mapping before deployment in any new jurisdiction.

notes.regulation: Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act (29 U.S.C. § 794d)
notes.note: Any educational technology procured or developed by a federally funded institution must conform to the Revised 508 Standards, which incorporate WCAG 2.0 Level AA. This applies to the code generation interface, output rendering, error messages, and any instructor dashboards. Non-compliance exposes institutions to OCR complaints and potential loss of federal contracts or grants.

cta.heading: Automate Your Education Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI code generation agent against FERPA, COPPA, and Section 508 requirements—so your institution stays compliant, students stay protected, and your team stays focused on learning outcomes.
